title: Tight Contact Structures and Torus Knots
speaker: Hyunki Min
abstract:
One of the fundamental problems in contact topology is to classify contact structures on a given 3-manifold. In particular, classifying contact structures on surgeries along a given knot has been very poorly studied. The only fully understood case so far is that of the unknot (lens spaces); for all other knots we have only partial results, or none at all. Several topological and algebraic tools have been developed to attack this problem.
In this talk, we discuss recent developments and the strategy for classifying tight contact structures on surgeries along torus knots. This is joint work with John Etnyre, Bülent Tosun, and Konstantinos Varvarezos.

proof_stages.label: Tight versus overtwisted contact structures
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins by recalling the overtwisted/tight dichotomy, the h-principle classification of overtwisted structures by plane fields, and why the hard classification problem is therefore the tight case.

proof_stages.label: Convex surfaces, sutures, and bypasses
proof_stages.summary: Giroux convex-surface theory is presented as a topological encoding of contact boundary data by dividing sets or sutures, with Honda bypass attachments providing the basic operation for changing this boundary data and enabling cut-and-paste classifications.

proof_stages.label: Prime-manifold and Seifert-fibered reduction
proof_stages.summary: Using connected-sum behavior, geometrization, and finiteness results tied to incompressible tori, the classification problem is narrowed to prime manifolds, especially Seifert fibered spaces over S^2 with three singular fibers as the tractable finite-family setting.

proof_stages.label: Normalized Seifert data and known exceptional cases
proof_stages.summary: The standard normalized surgery description with Seifert invariant e0 is introduced, together with known classifications for e0 not equal to -1 or -2 and the exceptional behavior at e0 = -1, -2 where non-fillability and non-completeness of contact invariants arise.

proof_stages.label: Fixed-knot surgery formulation
proof_stages.summary: Because broad Seifert classifications have stalled, the question is reframed as classifying tight contact structures on all rational surgeries on a fixed knot in S^3, with torus knots chosen since their surgeries are Seifert fibered and encounter the difficult e0 values.

proof_stages.label: Complement–solid-torus decomposition of torus-knot surgery
proof_stages.summary: A torus-knot surgery is decomposed into the knot complement, a Seifert fibration over the disk, glued to a solid torus whose tight contact structures are known, leaving the key problem of deciding which boundary-compatible gluings remain tight.

proof_stages.label: Floer contact invariants from tight and overtwisted surgeries
proof_stages.summary: The proposed computation accounts for tight structures produced both by surgery on the standard tight S^3 and by surgery on overtwisted S^3 along non-loose knots, using L-space-knot Floer simplicity, bordered pairing, and Spin^c/Alexander grading matching to detect nonvanishing contact invariants for negative surgeries.

examples.name: Hemisphere-dividing suture
examples.note: A surface divided by a suture into plus and minus regions, described as upper and lower hemispheres, makes the sutured-surface encoding of convex boundary data concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: he uses the hemisphere-dividing curve only to illustrate that a suture separates a surface into plus and minus regions

examples.name: Torus with two dividing curves
examples.note: The torus carrying two sutures/dividing curves is used only to visualize how convex-surface data splits a surface into positive and negative regions.
examples.engagement_reason: he cites the torus with two curves as another sutured-surface picture, saying only that the curves divide it into plus and minus regions

examples.name: Lens spaces as glued solid tori and unknot surgeries
examples.note: Lens spaces illustrate the convex-surface divide-and-conquer strategy, and later serve as the already-solved fixed-knot surgery case because they arise from surgeries on the unknot.
examples.engagement_reason: he sketches the lens-space classification strategy by decomposing L(p,q) into two solid tori, matching boundary sutures under the gluing map, and counting glued contact structures

passes.reason: Lens spaces form a systematic infinite genus-one family and are used as a known classified case of unknot surgery.
lens spaces form a systematic infinite family cited as known glued-solid-torus/unknot-surgery cases, not as counterexamples.
Lens spaces appear here as the low-complexity genus-one/unknot-surgery base case for classifications, not as counterexamples.
lens spaces form a systematic infinite family obtained by gluing two solid tori or by unknot surgery, used here as a standard classified case
lens spaces form a standard infinite family used as a known classified case rather than as a failure case
Lens spaces form a standard infinite family used here as decomposable and unknot-surgery examples, not as sporadic or limiting objects.
Lens spaces form a standard infinite family of 3-manifolds used here as a known classifiable case, not as a failure case.
Lens spaces have a genus-one decomposition as two solid tori and arise from unknot surgery, placing them at very low Heegaard complexity, but they are a systematic family.
Used as an already-classified base family, not as a refutation; lens spaces have a genus-one two-solid-torus/unknot-surgery minimal-complexity structure.
Lens spaces here are a systematic infinite family obtained by gluing two solid tori or by unknot surgery, used as a known classification case.
Lens spaces form the genus-one Heegaard/unknot-surgery family used as a successfully classified benchmark, not as a failure case.
Lens spaces form a systematic infinite family obtained from gluing solid tori or unknot surgery, used here as a previously classified model case.

examples.name: Zero surgery on the unknot giving S^1 x S^2
examples.note: The speaker uses zero surgery on the unknot as a simple trivial Seifert fibration, namely the product circle bundle over S^2, before discussing singular fibers.
